Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

{{Positionskarte+ |Egypt |caption=Map of Egypt |width=200 |float=right |places= {{Positionskarte~ |Egypt |label= '''Tebtunis''' |lat = 29/7/0/N |long = 30/45/0/E |region=EG-FYM |position=right }} }} The '''Tebtunis Lunar Calendar''' was discovered on March 10, 1931, under the direction of {{ill|Carlo Anti|ja|カルロ・アンティ|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tebtunis|ja|テブトゥニス|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the town, the temple of Soknebtynis (“Sobek, Lord of Tebtynis”) is located on the southern edge of the settlement. In front of the {{ill|Naos (Architecture)|ja|ナオス (建築)|lt=Naos|lt_ja=ナオス|12=draft|12=draft}} lies a small courtyard. The complex is surrounded by an {{ill|Enclosure Wall (Ancient Egypt)|ja|囲いの壁 (古代エジプト)|lt=Enclosure Wall|lt_ja=囲いの壁|de|Umfassungsmauer_(Altägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}, which also encloses other buildings, including a small subsidiary temple and numerous priest cells. == Background == The first excavations at this site were conducted in 1899/1900 by the two English papyrologists B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|12=draft|12=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|12=draft|12=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|12=draft|12=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|12=draft|12=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1. Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

{{Positionskarte+ |Egypt |caption=Map of Egypt |width=200 |float=right |places= {{Positionskarte~ |Egypt |label= '''Tebtunis''' |lat = 29/7/0/N |long = 30/45/0/E |region=EG-FYM |position=right }} }} The '''Tebtunis Lunar Calendar''' was discovered on March 10, 1931, under the direction of {{ill|Carlo Anti|ja|カルロ・アンティ|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tebtunis|ja|テブトゥニス|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the town, the temple of Soknebtynis (“Sobek, Lord of Tebtynis”) is located on the southern edge of the settlement. In front of the {{ill|Naos (Architecture)|ja|ナオス (建築)|lt=Naos|lt_ja=ナオス|12=draft|12=draft}} lies a small courtyard. The complex is surrounded by an {{ill|Enclosure Wall (Ancient Egypt)|ja|囲いの壁 (古代エジプト)|lt=Enclosure Wall|lt_ja=囲いの壁|de|Umfassungsmauer_(Altägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}, which also encloses other buildings, including a small subsidiary temple and numerous priest cells. == Background == The first excavations at this site were conducted in 1899/1900 by the two English papyrologists B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|12=draft|12=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|12=draft|12=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|12=draft|12=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|12=draft|12=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|12=draft|12=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1. Achet I|lt_ja=1. アシェト I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 23 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 10 2/3 hours | 1. Achet II | 23 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Hathor (month)|ja|フト・ヘル|Hathor (month)=Hathor (month)|lt=Hut-heru|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 30. Achet II | 21 November |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Sekhmet|ja|サクメット|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Ka-her-ka|ja|カー・ヘルカ|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 8 hours ({{ill|Winter Solstice|ja|冬至|lt=Winter Solstice|lt_ja=冬至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 30. Achet III | 21 December |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Schef-bedet|ja|シェフ・ベデット|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 29. Achet IV | 19 January |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-wer|ja|レケ・ウェル|12=draft|12=draft}} | 10 2/3 hours | {{ill|Peret (Ancient Egypt)|ja|ペレット|lt=29. Peret I|lt_ja=29. ペレット I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 18 February |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-nedjes|ja|レケ・ネジェス|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 28. Peret II | 19 March |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Mut (Egyptian Mythology)|ja|ムト|lt=Mut|lt_ja=ムト|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Renutet|ja|レヌテット|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 13 1/3 hours | 28. Peret III | 18 April |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Chonsu (month)|ja|コンス|de|Chonsu (Monat)|lt=Chonsu|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 27. Peret IV | 17 May |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Khenti-khet|ja|チェンティ・チェット|de|Chenti-chet|Khenti-khet=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 hours ({{ill|Summer Solstice|ja|夏至|lt=Summer Solstice|lt_ja=夏至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|ja|シェムウ|lt=27. Schemu I|lt_ja=27. シェムウ I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 June |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|ja|イペット・ヘメット|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|lt=Ipet-hemet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 26. Schemu II | 15 July |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Wepet-renpet|ja|ウェペット・レンペット|12=draft|12=draft}} | 13 1/3 hours | 26. Schemu III | 14 August |} == See Also == * {{ill|Ebers calendar|ja|エバーズ暦|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Rolf Krauss|ja|ロルフ・クラウス|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Sothis and Lunar Data: Studies on the Astronomical and Technical Chronology of Ancient Egypt.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Astronomical Algorithms. Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' 2nd revised edition. Barth, Leipzig et al. 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program. * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic Papyri from Tebtunis – Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Near Eastern Studies.'' Museum Tusculanum Press, University of Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 87-7289-280-3. * {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago Press, Chicago 1950. * Arthur M.F.W. Verhoogt: ''The Tebtunis Papyri at The Bancroft Library.'' In: ''Bancroftiana.'' Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

{{Positionskarte+ |Egypt |caption=Map of Egypt |width=200 |float=right |places= {{Positionskarte~ |Egypt |label= '''Tebtunis''' |lat = 29/7/0/N |long = 30/45/0/E |region=EG-FYM |position=right }} }} The '''Tebtunis Lunar Calendar''' was discovered on March 10, 1931, under the direction of {{ill|Carlo Anti|ja|カルロ・アンティ|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tebtunis|ja|テブトゥニス|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the town, the temple of Soknebtynis (“Sobek, Lord of Tebtynis”) is located on the southern edge of the settlement. In front of the {{ill|Naos (Architecture)|ja|ナオス (建築)|lt=Naos|lt_ja=ナオス|12=draft|12=draft}} lies a small courtyard. The complex is surrounded by an {{ill|Enclosure Wall (Ancient Egypt)|ja|囲いの壁 (古代エジプト)|lt=Enclosure Wall|lt_ja=囲いの壁|de|Umfassungsmauer_(Altägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}, which also encloses other buildings, including a small subsidiary temple and numerous priest cells. == Background == The first excavations at this site were conducted in 1899/1900 by the two English papyrologists B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|12=draft|12=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|12=draft|12=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|12=draft|12=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|12=draft|12=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1. Achet I|lt_ja=1. アシェト I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 23 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 10 2/3 hours | 1. Achet II | 23 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Hathor (month)|ja|フト・ヘル|Hathor (month)=Hathor (month)|lt=Hut-heru|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 30. Achet II | 21 November |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Sekhmet|ja|サクメット|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Ka-her-ka|ja|カー・ヘルカ|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 8 hours ({{ill|Winter Solstice|ja|冬至|lt=Winter Solstice|lt_ja=冬至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 30. Achet III | 21 December |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Schef-bedet|ja|シェフ・ベデット|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 29. Achet IV | 19 January |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-wer|ja|レケ・ウェル|12=draft|12=draft}} | 10 2/3 hours | {{ill|Peret (Ancient Egypt)|ja|ペレット|lt=29. Peret I|lt_ja=29. ペレット I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 18 February |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-nedjes|ja|レケ・ネジェス|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 28. Peret II | 19 March |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Mut (Egyptian Mythology)|ja|ムト|lt=Mut|lt_ja=ムト|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Renutet|ja|レヌテット|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 13 1/3 hours | 28. Peret III | 18 April |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Chonsu (month)|ja|コンス|de|Chonsu (Monat)|lt=Chonsu|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 27. Peret IV | 17 May |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Khenti-khet|ja|チェンティ・チェット|de|Chenti-chet|Khenti-khet=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 hours ({{ill|Summer Solstice|ja|夏至|lt=Summer Solstice|lt_ja=夏至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|ja|シェムウ|lt=27. Schemu I|lt_ja=27. シェムウ I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 June |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|ja|イペット・ヘメット|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|lt=Ipet-hemet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 26. Schemu II | 15 July |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Wepet-renpet|ja|ウェペット・レンペット|12=draft|12=draft}} | 13 1/3 hours | 26. Schemu III | 14 August |} == See Also == * {{ill|Ebers calendar|ja|エバーズ暦|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Rolf Krauss|ja|ロルフ・クラウス|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Sothis and Lunar Data: Studies on the Astronomical and Technical Chronology of Ancient Egypt.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Astronomical Algorithms. Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' 2nd revised edition. Barth, Leipzig et al. 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program. * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic Papyri from Tebtunis – Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Near Eastern Studies.'' Museum Tusculanum Press, University of Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 87-7289-280-3. * {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago Press, Chicago 1950. * Arthur M.F.W. Verhoogt: ''The Tebtunis Papyri at The Bancroft Library.'' In: ''Bancroftiana.'' Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Positionskarte+ |Egypt |caption=Map of Egypt |width=200 |float=right |places= {{Positionskarte~ |Egypt |label= '''Tebtunis''' |lat = 29/7/0/N |long = 30/45/0/E |region=EG-FYM |position=right }} }} The '''Tebtunis Lunar Calendar''' was discovered on March 10, 1931, under the direction of {{ill|Carlo Anti|ja|カルロ・アンティ|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tebtunis|ja|テブトゥニス|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the town, the temple of Soknebtynis (“Sobek, Lord of Tebtynis”) is located on the southern edge of the settlement. In front of the {{ill|Naos (Architecture)|ja|ナオス (建築)|lt=Naos|lt_ja=ナオス|12=draft|12=draft}} lies a small courtyard. The complex is surrounded by an {{ill|Enclosure Wall (Ancient Egypt)|ja|囲いの壁 (古代エジプト)|lt=Enclosure Wall|lt_ja=囲いの壁|de|Umfassungsmauer_(Altägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}, which also encloses other buildings, including a small subsidiary temple and numerous priest cells. == Background == The first excavations at this site were conducted in 1899/1900 by the two English papyrologists B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|12=draft|12=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|12=draft|12=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|12=draft|12=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|12=draft|12=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1. Achet I|lt_ja=1. アシェト I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 23 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 10 2/3 hours | 1. Achet II | 23 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Hathor (month)|ja|フト・ヘル|Hathor (month)=Hathor (month)|lt=Hut-heru|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 30. Achet II | 21 November |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Sekhmet|ja|サクメット|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Ka-her-ka|ja|カー・ヘルカ|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 8 hours ({{ill|Winter Solstice|ja|冬至|lt=Winter Solstice|lt_ja=冬至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 30. Achet III | 21 December |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Schef-bedet|ja|シェフ・ベデット|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 29. Achet IV | 19 January |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-wer|ja|レケ・ウェル|12=draft|12=draft}} | 10 2/3 hours | {{ill|Peret (Ancient Egypt)|ja|ペレット|lt=29. Peret I|lt_ja=29. ペレット I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 18 February |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-nedjes|ja|レケ・ネジェス|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 28. Peret II | 19 March |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Mut (Egyptian Mythology)|ja|ムト|lt=Mut|lt_ja=ムト|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Renutet|ja|レヌテット|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 13 1/3 hours | 28. Peret III | 18 April |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Chonsu (month)|ja|コンス|de|Chonsu (Monat)|lt=Chonsu|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 27. Peret IV | 17 May |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Khenti-khet|ja|チェンティ・チェット|de|Chenti-chet|Khenti-khet=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 hours ({{ill|Summer Solstice|ja|夏至|lt=Summer Solstice|lt_ja=夏至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|ja|シェムウ|lt=27. Schemu I|lt_ja=27. シェムウ I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 June |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|ja|イペット・ヘメット|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|lt=Ipet-hemet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 26. Schemu II | 15 July |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Wepet-renpet|ja|ウェペット・レンペット|12=draft|12=draft}} | 13 1/3 hours | 26. Schemu III | 14 August |} == See Also == * {{ill|Ebers calendar|ja|エバーズ暦|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Rolf Krauss|ja|ロルフ・クラウス|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Sothis and Lunar Data: Studies on the Astronomical and Technical Chronology of Ancient Egypt.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Astronomical Algorithms. Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' 2nd revised edition. Barth, Leipzig et al. 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program. * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic Papyri from Tebtunis – Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Near Eastern Studies.'' Museum Tusculanum Press, University of Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 87-7289-280-3. * {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago Press, Chicago 1950. * Arthur M.F.W. Verhoogt: ''The Tebtunis Papyri at The Bancroft Library.'' In: ''Bancroftiana.'' Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

Barth, Leipzig et al. 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program. * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic Papyri from Tebtunis – Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Near Eastern Studies.'' Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

{{Positionskarte+ |Egypt |caption=Map of Egypt |width=200 |float=right |places= {{Positionskarte~ |Egypt |label= '''Tebtunis''' |lat = 29/7/0/N |long = 30/45/0/E |region=EG-FYM |position=right }} }} The '''Tebtunis Lunar Calendar''' was discovered on March 10, 1931, under the direction of {{ill|Carlo Anti|ja|カルロ・アンティ|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tebtunis|ja|テブトゥニス|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the town, the temple of Soknebtynis (“Sobek, Lord of Tebtynis”) is located on the southern edge of the settlement. In front of the {{ill|Naos (Architecture)|ja|ナオス (建築)|lt=Naos|lt_ja=ナオス|12=draft|12=draft}} lies a small courtyard. The complex is surrounded by an {{ill|Enclosure Wall (Ancient Egypt)|ja|囲いの壁 (古代エジプト)|lt=Enclosure Wall|lt_ja=囲いの壁|de|Umfassungsmauer_(Altägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}, which also encloses other buildings, including a small subsidiary temple and numerous priest cells. == Background == The first excavations at this site were conducted in 1899/1900 by the two English papyrologists B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|12=draft|12=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|12=draft|12=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|12=draft|12=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|12=draft|12=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1. Peret I|lt_ja=29. ペレット I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 18 February |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-nedjes|ja|レケ・ネジェス|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 28. Peret II | 19 March |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Mut (Egyptian Mythology)|ja|ムト|lt=Mut|lt_ja=ムト|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Renutet|ja|レヌテット|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 13 1/3 hours | 28. Peret IV | 17 May |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Khenti-khet|ja|チェンティ・チェット|de|Chenti-chet|Khenti-khet=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 hours ({{ill|Summer Solstice|ja|夏至|lt=Summer Solstice|lt_ja=夏至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|ja|シェムウ|lt=27. Schemu I|lt_ja=27. シェムウ I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 June |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|ja|イペット・ヘメット|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|lt=Ipet-hemet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 26. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|12=draft|12=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|12=draft|12=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|12=draft|12=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|12=draft|12=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1. Achet I|lt_ja=1. アシェト I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 23 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 10 2/3 hours | 1. Achet II | 23 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Hathor (month)|ja|フト・ヘル|Hathor (month)=Hathor (month)|lt=Hut-heru|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 30. Achet II | 21 November |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Sekhmet|ja|サクメット|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Ka-her-ka|ja|カー・ヘルカ|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 8 hours ({{ill|Winter Solstice|ja|冬至|lt=Winter Solstice|lt_ja=冬至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 30. Achet III | 21 December |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Schef-bedet|ja|シェフ・ベデット|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 29. Achet IV | 19 January |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-wer|ja|レケ・ウェル|12=draft|12=draft}} | 10 2/3 hours | {{ill|Peret (Ancient Egypt)|ja|ペレット|lt=29. Peret I|lt_ja=29. ペレット I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 18 February |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-nedjes|ja|レケ・ネジェス|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 28. Peret II | 19 March |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Mut (Egyptian Mythology)|ja|ムト|lt=Mut|lt_ja=ムト|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Renutet|ja|レヌテット|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 13 1/3 hours | 28. Peret III | 18 April |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Chonsu (month)|ja|コンス|de|Chonsu (Monat)|lt=Chonsu|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 27. Peret IV | 17 May |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Khenti-khet|ja|チェンティ・チェット|de|Chenti-chet|Khenti-khet=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 hours ({{ill|Summer Solstice|ja|夏至|lt=Summer Solstice|lt_ja=夏至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|ja|シェムウ|lt=27. Schemu I|lt_ja=27. シェムウ I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 June |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|ja|イペット・ヘメット|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|lt=Ipet-hemet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 26. Schemu III | 14 August |} == See Also == * {{ill|Ebers calendar|ja|エバーズ暦|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Rolf Krauss|ja|ロルフ・クラウス|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Sothis and Lunar Data: Studies on the Astronomical and Technical Chronology of Ancient Egypt.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Astronomical Algorithms. Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' 2nd revised edition. Barth, Leipzig et al. 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program. * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic Papyri from Tebtunis – Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Near Eastern Studies.'' Museum Tusculanum Press, University of Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 87-7289-280-3. * {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago Press, Chicago 1950. * Arthur M.F.W. Verhoogt: ''The Tebtunis Papyri at The Bancroft Library.'' In: ''Bancroftiana.'' Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

{{Positionskarte+ |Egypt |caption=Map of Egypt |width=200 |float=right |places= {{Positionskarte~ |Egypt |label= '''Tebtunis''' |lat = 29/7/0/N |long = 30/45/0/E |region=EG-FYM |position=right }} }} The '''Tebtunis Lunar Calendar''' was discovered on March 10, 1931, under the direction of {{ill|Carlo Anti|ja|カルロ・アンティ|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tebtunis|ja|テブトゥニス|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the town, the temple of Soknebtynis (“Sobek, Lord of Tebtynis”) is located on the southern edge of the settlement. In front of the {{ill|Naos (Architecture)|ja|ナオス (建築)|lt=Naos|lt_ja=ナオス|12=draft|12=draft}} lies a small courtyard. The complex is surrounded by an {{ill|Enclosure Wall (Ancient Egypt)|ja|囲いの壁 (古代エジプト)|lt=Enclosure Wall|lt_ja=囲いの壁|de|Umfassungsmauer_(Altägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}, which also encloses other buildings, including a small subsidiary temple and numerous priest cells. == Background == The first excavations at this site were conducted in 1899/1900 by the two English papyrologists B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|12=draft|12=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|12=draft|12=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|12=draft|12=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|12=draft|12=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1. Achet I|lt_ja=1. アシェト I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 23 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 10 2/3 hours | 1. Achet II | 23 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Hathor (month)|ja|フト・ヘル|Hathor (month)=Hathor (month)|lt=Hut-heru|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 30. Achet II | 21 November |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Sekhmet|ja|サクメット|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Ka-her-ka|ja|カー・ヘルカ|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 8 hours ({{ill|Winter Solstice|ja|冬至|lt=Winter Solstice|lt_ja=冬至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 30. Achet III | 21 December |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Schef-bedet|ja|シェフ・ベデット|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 29. Achet IV | 19 January |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-wer|ja|レケ・ウェル|12=draft|12=draft}} | 10 2/3 hours | {{ill|Peret (Ancient Egypt)|ja|ペレット|lt=29. Peret I|lt_ja=29. ペレット I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 18 February |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-nedjes|ja|レケ・ネジェス|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 28. Peret II | 19 March |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Mut (Egyptian Mythology)|ja|ムト|lt=Mut|lt_ja=ムト|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Renutet|ja|レヌテット|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 13 1/3 hours | 28. Peret III | 18 April |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Chonsu (month)|ja|コンス|de|Chonsu (Monat)|lt=Chonsu|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 27. Peret IV | 17 May |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Khenti-khet|ja|チェンティ・チェット|de|Chenti-chet|Khenti-khet=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 hours ({{ill|Summer Solstice|ja|夏至|lt=Summer Solstice|lt_ja=夏至|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|ja|シェムウ|lt=27. Schemu I|lt_ja=27. シェムウ I|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 June |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|ja|イペット・ヘメット|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|lt=Ipet-hemet|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 26. Schemu III | 14 August |} == See Also == * {{ill|Ebers calendar|ja|エバーズ暦|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Rolf Krauss|ja|ロルフ・クラウス|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Sothis and Lunar Data: Studies on the Astronomical and Technical Chronology of Ancient Egypt.'' Achet II | 23 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Hathor (month)|ja|フト・ヘル|Hathor (month)=Hathor (month)|lt=Hut-heru|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 30. Achet II | 21 November |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Sekhmet|ja|サクメット|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Ka-her-ka|ja|カー・ヘルカ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 8 hours ({{ill|Winter Solstice|ja|冬至|lt=Winter Solstice|lt_ja=冬至|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 30. Schemu III | 14 August |} == See Also == * {{ill|Ebers calendar|ja|エバーズ暦|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Rolf Krauss|ja|ロルフ・クラウス|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Sothis and Lunar Data: Studies on the Astronomical and Technical Chronology of Ancient Egypt.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Astronomical Algorithms. Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|Gregorian Calendar|" to "|Gregorian Calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|"
revision.text:
{{Positionskarte+ |Egypt |caption=Map of Egypt |width=200 |float=right |places= {{Positionskarte~ |Egypt |label= '''Tebtunis''' |lat = 29/7/0/N |long = 30/45/0/E |region=EG-FYM |position=right }} }} The '''Tebtunis Lunar Calendar''' was discovered on March 10, 1931, under the direction of {{ill|Carlo Anti|ja|カルロ・アンティ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tebtunis|ja|テブトゥニス|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the town, the temple of Soknebtynis (“Sobek, Lord of Tebtynis”) is located on the southern edge of the settlement. In front of the {{ill|Naos (Architecture)|ja|ナオス (建築)|lt=Naos|lt_ja=ナオス|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} lies a small courtyard. The complex is surrounded by an {{ill|Enclosure Wall (Ancient Egypt)|ja|囲いの壁 (古代エジプト)|lt=Enclosure Wall|lt_ja=囲いの壁|de|Umfassungsmauer_(Altägypten)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, which also encloses other buildings, including a small subsidiary temple and numerous priest cells. == Background == The first excavations at this site were conducted in 1899/1900 by the two English papyrologists B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Achet III | 21 December |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Schef-bedet|ja|シェフ・ベデット|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 9 1/3 hours | 29. Achet IV | 19 January |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-wer|ja|レケ・ウェル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 10 2/3 hours | {{ill|Peret (Ancient Egypt)|ja|ペレット|lt=29. Peret I|lt_ja=29. ペレット I|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 18 February |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Rekeh-nedjes|ja|レケ・ネジェス|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 28. Peret II | 19 March |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Mut (Egyptian Mythology)|ja|ムト|lt=Mut|lt_ja=ムト|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (instead of {{ill|Renutet|ja|レヌテット|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) | 13 1/3 hours | 28. Peret III | 18 April |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Chonsu (month)|ja|コンス|de|Chonsu (Monat)|lt=Chonsu|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 27. Peret IV | 17 May |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Khenti-khet|ja|チェンティ・チェット|de|Chenti-chet|Khenti-khet=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 hours ({{ill|Summer Solstice|ja|夏至|lt=Summer Solstice|lt_ja=夏至|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|ja|シェムウ|lt=27. Schemu I|lt_ja=27. シェムウ I|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 16 June |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|ja|イペット・ヘメット|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|lt=Ipet-hemet|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 14 2/3 hours | 26. Schemu II | 15 July |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | 1. {{ill|Wepet-renpet|ja|ウェペット・レンペット|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | 13 1/3 hours | 26. Schemu III | 14 August |} == See Also == * {{ill|Ebers calendar|ja|エバーズ暦|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Rolf Krauss|ja|ロルフ・クラウス|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Sothis and Lunar Data: Studies on the Astronomical and Technical Chronology of Ancient Egypt.'' Volume 107, 1994, pp. 4–7. == Notes == == Citations == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Calendar (Antiquity)]] [[Category:Astronomical Data Collection]] [[Category:Historical Work (Astronomy)]] [[Category:Translated from German with ambiguous and strange interlanguage links]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Tebtunis lunar calendar]] [[de:Tebtunis-Mondkalender]] [[ja:テブトゥニス月暦]]

{{Positionskarte+ |Egypt |caption=Map of Egypt |width=200 |float=right |places= {{Positionskarte~ |Egypt |label= '''Tebtunis''' |lat = 29/7/0/N |long = 30/45/0/E |region=EG-FYM |position=right }} }} The '''Tebtunis Lunar Calendar''' was discovered on March 10, 1931, under the direction of {{ill|Carlo Anti|ja|カルロ・アンティ|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Tebtunis|ja|テブトゥニス|qq=draft}}. In the town, the temple of Soknebtynis (“Sobek, Lord of Tebtynis”) is located on the southern edge of the settlement. In front of the {{ill|Naos (Architecture)|ja|ナオス (建築)|lt=Naos|lt_ja=ナオス|qq=draft}} lies a small courtyard. The complex is surrounded by an {{ill|Enclosure Wall (Ancient Egypt)|ja|囲いの壁 (古代エジプト)|lt=Enclosure Wall|lt_ja=囲いの壁|de|Umfassungsmauer_(Altägypten)|qq=draft}}, which also encloses other buildings, including a small subsidiary temple and numerous priest cells. == Background == The first excavations at this site were conducted in 1899/1900 by the two English papyrologists B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|qq=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|qq=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|qq=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|qq=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|qq=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|qq=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|qq=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|qq=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|qq=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|qq=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|qq=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|qq=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|qq=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|qq=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|qq=draft}}. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt. They uncovered parts of the Soknebtynis temple and the surrounding settlement, discovering numerous {{ill|Ancient Greek|ja|古代ギリシア語|lt=Greek|lt_ja=ギリシア語|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Demotic language|ja|デモティック語|lt=Demotic|lt_ja=デモティック|qq=draft}} {{ill|Papyrology|ja|パピロロジー|lt=Papyri|lt_ja=パピロロジー|qq=draft}} – the actual goal of their excavations. In the division of finds, most of the demotic papyri went to {{ill|Cairo|ja|カイロ|qq=draft}}, while the Greek ones and those still in the boxes were initially sent for processing to {{ill|University of Oxford|ja|オックスフォード大学|lt=Oxford|lt_ja=オックスフォード|qq=draft}} and later – after the death of Grenfell and Hunt – to the {{ill|Bancroft Library|ja|バンクロフト図書館|de|Bancroft Library|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|University of California|ja|カリフォルニア大学|qq=draft}}; the last boxes that had been overlooked earlier have only recently arrived there. The analysis of these Tebtunis papyri is handled by the {{ill|Center for the Tebtunis Papyri|ja|テブトゥニス・パピルス研究センター|de|Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|de=Center_for_the_Tebtunis_Papyri|qq=draft}}, founded in 2000. In contrast, literary texts in demotic as well as hieratic script were sent to {{ill|Copenhagen|ja|コペンハーゲン|qq=draft}}. They undoubtedly originate from the library of the Soknebtynis temple. == The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar == [[File:Jahreszeiten99 DE2.jpg|right|400px|Seasonal diagram, overview of the reference lines of the Earth’s orbit]] The construction of the temple complex is estimated to date from the second century BC. Accordingly, the Tebtunis calendar, which contains detailed information on numerous festivals as well as details regarding the {{ill|Hours of the Night|ja|夜の時間|lt=Hours of the Night|lt_ja=夜の時間|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Hours of the Day|ja|日の時間|lt=Hours of the Day|lt_ja=日の時間|qq=draft}}, is dated to that period. The hypothesis expressed by {{ill|Alan Gardiner|ja|アラン・ガーディナー|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|ja|リチャード・アンソニー・パーカー|qq=draft}} that the {{ill|Egyptian Calendar|ja|エジプト暦|lt=Egyptian Calendar|lt_ja=エジプト暦|qq=draft}} had changed its annual form at the latest since the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|qq=draft}} is further confirmed by the Tebtunis calendar. Notably, the ancient month names of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|ja|古代エジプトの月暦|lt=Lunar Calendar|lt_ja=月暦|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qq=Lunar Calendar (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}} are used, while in the main calendar the newly introduced month names were employed. Two lunar month names in the Tebtunis Lunar Calendar are dedicated to other deities. Furthermore, the month {{ill|Koiak|ja|カー・ヘルカ|de|Ka-her-ka|lt=Ka-her-ka|qq=draft}} was advanced to the position of {{ill|Menchet (month)|ja|メンシェット|lt=Menchet|lt_ja=メンシェット|de|Menchet (Monat)|qq=draft}}. {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |+ '''The Tebtunis Lunar Calendar in Comparison to the Egyptian Calendar around 132 BC''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} ! Tebtunis Lunar Calendar ! Length of Day
(Book of the Hours of the Day) ! Egyptian Calendar ! DateCf. {{ill|Jean Meeus|ja|ジャン・ミューズ|qq=draft}} ''Astronomical Algorithms – Applications for Ephemeris Tool 4,5.'' Barth, Leipzig 2000, ISBN 3-335-00400-0, ''Ephemeris Tool 4,5 Calculation Program''.Data of the {{ill|Gregorian Calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|ja|グレゴリオ暦|lt=Gregorian Calendar|lt_ja=グレゴリオ暦|qq=draft}}. To determine the reference values in the {{ill|proleptic calendar|ja|先取暦|qq=draft}} two days must be added. |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | 1. {{ill|Techi|ja|テヒ|qq=draft}} | 12 hours ({{ill|Equinox|ja|春分・秋分|lt=Equinox|lt_ja=春分・秋分|qq=draft}}) | {{ill|Achet|ja|アシェト|lt=1.
